- `search_files()` is the objects-creating method. Iterate through something and return instantiated `BaseModel` subclasses. An `instantiate_from_dir/package/file/whatever()` might be clearer. Or `create_....()`?

- `scan()` is what then gets called (by what? I would expect it to be called in the `__init__()`?) to read the file and extract info from it. Would `.extract_info()` be a clearer name? Depends also on what it actually does.

Type is NOT optional. Every resource/child name will have an associated type. 
Namespace is optional, but does not inherit from the its parent. This allows us to differentiate between the following ids:
`/subscriptions/sub/resourcegroups/group/providers/namespace/rootType/rootName/childType/childName`
and
`/subscriptions/sub/resourcegroups/group/providers/namespace/rootType/rootName/providers/childNamespace/childType/childName`
If the namespace was inherited, and the id was reformed, it becomes unclear whether the inherited namespace would be included in the resulting id without checking the child's parent.

not sure we should use `DATADOG_SERVICE_NAME`. `pymemcache` is not an entry point (in general) and if someone sets the value as their application name (i.e. `intake-api`), the latency and the breakdown for `pymemcache` will not be computed (you don't see the req/s in the service page etc...).

I think it should be just `memcachedx.SERVICE`. People then will use the `Pin` object to change the default value. You can attach the Pin to `pymemcache` package (we support that) so when a new wrapper is created you will inherits the `Pin` (see the comment below).

-  `x1` si `cond1 == True`
-  `x2` si `cond1 == False and cond2 == True`
-  `x3` si `cond1 == False and cond2 == False and cond3 == True`.

I'm a bit uncomfortable with this.  The `aberrations` parameter for `OpticalPSF` is given in units of waves.  I think it might be a bit less of a burden on the user to keep the same definition here, but have the user also give a fiducial wavelength at which the aberrations are defined.

In fact, we could add `lambda` and `diam` to `OpticalPSF` (and maybe even deprecate `lam_over_diam` which is a bit unwieldy, imo), in which case we could have the two classes take exactly the same arguments.  

Of course this one would have a proper wavelength dependence given those parameters, and the other would only be correct at a single wavelength.  But I think making the arguments the same would improve the API.

I was reacting as much to the changes propagated to the tests as to the code itself, which made me question the optimization:

- Do we  expect `to_pb` to be called frequently for a given filter?
- Is there a chance that `to_pb` might never get called for some filters?
- Is the cost of encoding inside `to_pb` if it were called more than once measurable?
